stages.title: Foundations
stages.summary: Start here. Understand what backend actually is and how the web moves data from one computer to another.

quiz.q: Where does backend code run?
quiz.explain: Backend code runs on a server you control — not on the user's device — which is why it can safely hold secrets and talk to databases.

quiz.q: Why can't you put your database password in frontend JavaScript?
options: Anyone can open DevTools and read it
quiz.explain: Everything shipped to the browser is public. Anyone can read it in DevTools — so secrets only ever live on the backend.

resources.title: Backend Developer Roadmap
resources.note: The classic visual roadmap — a great companion to this course.

resources.title: What is a web server?
resources.note: MDN's plain-English intro.

resources.title: Server-side vs client-side code
resources.note: MDN's server-side first steps.

quiz.q: Which concern belongs to the backend?
options: Validating a password against the database
quiz.explain: Checking credentials against stored data is a backend job — it needs the database and must not be trusted to the client.

quiz.q: Your frontend already validates that age >= 18. Should the backend check it too?
options: Yes — an attacker can skip the frontend entirely
quiz.explain: Frontend validation is UX. An attacker can send a raw request with any value, so the backend must re-validate anything that matters.

resources.title: Never trust the client
resources.note: OWASP on why client-side validation is never enough.

quiz.q: What is a 'packet'?
options: A small chunk of data sent over the network
quiz.explain: Data is broken into small packets that travel independently and are reassembled at the destination.

quiz.q: Which protocol guarantees packets arrive in order and re-sends lost ones?
quiz.explain: TCP provides reliable, ordered delivery. UDP is fire-and-forget with no such guarantees.

resources.title: What happens when you type a URL?
resources.note: The legendary deep-dive — every layer, in obsessive detail.

quiz.q: What does DNS do?
options: Turns a domain name into an IP address
quiz.explain: DNS is the internet's phone book: it resolves human names like example.com to machine IP addresses.

quiz.q: You change a DNS record but some users still hit the old server. Why?
options: The old answer is still cached until its TTL expires
You need to restart the server
quiz.explain: DNS answers are cached per their TTL. Lower the TTL *before* a migration so changes propagate quickly.

resources.title: What is DNS?
resources.note: Cloudflare's DNS explainer — clear and visual.

resources.title: How DNS works (comic)
resources.note: A genuinely fun cartoon walkthrough.

quiz.q: In the client–server model, who starts the conversation?
quiz.explain: Clients initiate requests; servers listen and respond. The server doesn't call the client out of the blue.

quiz.q: HTTP is 'stateless'. How do you stay logged in across requests?
options: The client re-sends a token/cookie on every request
quiz.explain: Each request is independent, so the client attaches proof of identity (cookie or token) every single time.

stages.title: The Web & HTTP
stages.summary: HTTP is the language every backend speaks. Learn the request/response cycle inside out.

quiz.q: What does the 'S' in HTTPS add?
quiz.explain: HTTPS wraps HTTP in TLS so data can't be read or tampered with between client and server.

quiz.q: Which part of a request carries JSON you POST?
quiz.explain: The request body carries the payload (like JSON). The method and path describe what and where.

resources.title: HTTP Messages
resources.note: The exact anatomy of requests and responses.

resources.title: HTTPie — a friendly HTTP client
resources.note: Great for poking at APIs from the terminal.

quiz.q: Which method should be safe and not change data?
quiz.explain: GET is meant to be read-only and safe — it should never change server state.

quiz.q: Which method is NOT idempotent?
quiz.explain: Two identical POSTs create two resources. GET/PUT/DELETE can be repeated with the same end result.

quiz.q: What does a 401 mean?
quiz.explain: 401 Unauthorized means the request lacks valid authentication. 403 means you're authenticated but not allowed.

quiz.q: A user sends invalid JSON. Which family of status code should you return?
quiz.explain: 4xx = the client's fault. Bad input is a 400. 5xx would wrongly blame your server.

resources.title: HTTP Cats
resources.note: Every status code, as a cat. Weirdly effective for memorising them.

quiz.q: How does a browser send a cookie back to the server?
options: In the Cookie request header automatically
quiz.explain: Once set, the browser automatically attaches cookies to matching requests via the Cookie header.

quiz.q: Which cookie flag stops JavaScript from reading a session cookie?
quiz.explain: HttpOnly hides the cookie from document.cookie, protecting it from XSS token theft.

quiz.q: What key problem does HTTP/2 multiplexing solve?
options: Head-of-line blocking at the HTTP layer
quiz.explain: HTTP/2 sends many streams over one TCP connection, so one slow response no longer blocks the others.

quiz.q: HTTP/3 is built on which transport protocol?
quiz.explain: HTTP/3 runs on QUIC over UDP, which removes TCP's head-of-line blocking and speeds up connection setup.

resources.title: HTTP/2 explained
resources.note: Free, thorough, written by the curl author.

stages.title: Servers & Runtimes
stages.summary: What a server really is, how it handles many users at once, and the models that make it fast.

quiz.q: What is a 'port'?
options: A numbered channel on a machine that a service listens on
quiz.explain: A port is a numbered endpoint (like 443 for HTTPS) so one machine can run many services at once.

resources.title: Beej's Guide to Network Programming
resources.note: If you ever want to see the raw sockets under every framework.

quiz.q: Node.js handles many connections mostly by…
quiz.explain: Node uses a single-threaded event loop with non-blocking I/O to juggle thousands of connections.

quiz.q: Why is heavy CPU work dangerous in Node.js?
options: It blocks the single event loop, freezing all other requests
quiz.explain: The event loop is one thread. A long synchronous computation stalls every other pending request.

resources.title: What the heck is the event loop anyway? (video)
resources.note: Philip Roberts' famous talk — the clearest explanation ever given.

resources.title: Go Concurrency Patterns (video)
resources.note: Rob Pike on goroutines and channels.

quiz.q: A 'cold start' happens when…
options: A serverless function spins up from idle
quiz.explain: Serverless functions that have been idle must boot a fresh environment — that delay is the cold start.

resources.title: Serverless Architectures
resources.note: Martin Fowler's definitive, balanced overview.

stages.title: APIs
stages.summary: APIs are how backends expose their power. Design them well and everything else gets easier.

quiz.q: An API is best described as…
options: A contract for how software talks to software
quiz.explain: An API is a defined contract: given this request, expect this response — regardless of internal implementation.

resources.title: Public APIs to practise with
resources.note: A huge list of free APIs — great for building things.

quiz.q: Which URL is the most RESTful for one user?
quiz.explain: REST models resources as nouns with IDs: GET /users/5. The verb comes from the HTTP method, not the URL.

resources.title: Microsoft REST API Guidelines
resources.note: A real, battle-tested style guide you can copy.

resources.title: Roy Fielding's REST dissertation (ch. 5)
resources.note: The original definition of REST, from the source.

quiz.q: Serialization means…
options: Turning in-memory objects into a transferable format like JSON
quiz.explain: Serialization converts objects to a portable string (JSON); deserialization parses it back into objects.

resources.title: Zod — TypeScript schema validation
resources.note: The standard way to validate untrusted JSON in TS.

quiz.q: Why is offset pagination (?page=500) slow on big tables?
options: The database must scan and discard all preceding rows
quiz.explain: OFFSET 10000 makes the DB walk past 10,000 rows before returning any. Cursor pagination seeks directly instead.

quiz.q: A breaking API change means…
options: Removing or renaming a field clients depend on
quiz.explain: Adding optional things is safe. Removing/renaming/changing types breaks existing clients — that needs a new version.

resources.title: Stripe API design
resources.note: Widely considered the gold standard — study how they version and paginate.

resources.title: Cursor-based pagination explained
resources.note: Slack's engineering write-up on why they moved to cursors.

resources.title: OpenAPI Specification
resources.note: Describe your API formally — get docs and clients for free.

quiz.q: A key advantage of GraphQL over REST is…
options: Clients request exactly the fields they need
quiz.explain: GraphQL lets the client ask for precisely the fields it wants in one request, avoiding over/under-fetching.

quiz.q: Why use WebSockets instead of polling?
options: Persistent two-way connection for instant push
quiz.explain: WebSockets keep an open, bidirectional channel so the server can push updates instantly without repeated requests.

resources.title: Server-Sent Events
resources.note: The simpler one-way alternative — often all you need.

quiz.q: How do you verify a webhook really came from the sender?
options: Verify the HMAC signature in the header using a shared secret
quiz.explain: Providers sign the raw body with a shared secret. Recompute the HMAC and compare — otherwise anyone can forge events.

quiz.q: Why must a webhook handler be idempotent?
options: Providers retry on failure, so the same event can arrive twice
quiz.explain: Webhook delivery is at-least-once. Store the event ID and skip duplicates or you'll process the same payment twice.

resources.title: Stripe webhooks guide
resources.note: The reference implementation — signatures, retries, idempotency.

resources.title: webhook.site — inspect webhooks
resources.note: Instantly see what a provider is actually sending you.

stages.title: Databases
stages.summary: Where your data lives. Model it well, query it fast, and keep it consistent.

quiz.q: A relational (SQL) database is a great default when…
options: Your data is structured and consistency matters
You have no schema at all
quiz.explain: SQL shines with structured, related data and strong consistency guarantees via transactions.

resources.title: PostgreSQL Tutorial
resources.note: Learn the database you'll most likely use.

resources.title: SQLBolt — learn SQL interactively
resources.note: Best free hands-on SQL intro.

quiz.q: Normalization mainly reduces…
quiz.explain: Normalization removes redundant data so a fact lives in one place, preventing inconsistent updates.

quiz.q: A JOIN is used to…
quiz.explain: JOINs combine rows across tables using related keys, e.g. orders joined to their users.

quiz.q: You loop over 100 users and query each one's orders. What's the bug?
options: The N+1 query problem — use a JOIN or a single IN query
quiz.explain: That's 101 round trips. Fetch it in one query with a JOIN or WHERE user_id IN (...).

resources.title: A visual explanation of SQL joins
PostgreSQL Exercises
resources.note: Practise real queries against a real schema.

quiz.q: An index speeds up reads but…
options: Slows writes and uses more storage
quiz.explain: Indexes make lookups fast but must be updated on every write and take extra space — a classic trade-off.

quiz.q: You have an index on (user_id, created_at). Which query does it NOT help much?
quiz.explain: A composite index is sorted by its leftmost column first. Without user_id, it can't seek efficiently by created_at alone.

resources.title: Use The Index, Luke!
resources.note: THE free resource on SQL indexing. If you read one thing, read this.

quiz.q: In an EXPLAIN plan, seeing 'Seq Scan' on a huge table usually means…
quiz.explain: A sequential scan reads every row. On a large table filtered by a column, that usually means the index is missing or unusable.

quiz.q: Why can wrapping a column in a function (WHERE LOWER(email) = ...) kill an index?
options: The index stores raw values, not the function's output — so it can't be used
quiz.explain: The index is sorted on the raw column. Transform the column and the DB can no longer seek — unless you add an expression index.

resources.title: explain.dahibi.com — visualize a plan
resources.note: Paste an EXPLAIN plan, get a readable diagram.

resources.title: Use The Index, Luke — slow queries

quiz.q: The 'A' in ACID (Atomicity) means…
quiz.explain: Atomicity means a transaction fully commits or fully rolls back — never half-applied.

quiz.q: Two requests both read stock=1 and both sell the item. What's this called?
quiz.explain: Classic race condition. Fix with a transaction + row lock (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) or an atomic conditional UPDATE.

resources.title: Jepsen — consistency models
resources.note: The definitive map of isolation/consistency levels.

quiz.q: A database migration is…
options: A versioned, repeatable change to the schema
quiz.explain: Migrations are version-controlled scripts that evolve your schema so every environment stays in sync.

resources.title: Safe schema migrations
resources.note: How to change a schema without downtime.

quiz.q: Why use a connection pool instead of connecting per request?
options: Connections are expensive to open and databases cap how many exist
quiz.explain: Opening a DB connection costs a TCP + auth handshake, and Postgres allows only a limited number. A pool reuses a small set.

quiz.q: You run 10 app instances with pool size 20 each, against Postgres with max_connections=100. What happens?
options: Up to 200 connections are demanded — the DB rejects connections
quiz.explain: Pool sizes multiply by instance count. 10 × 20 = 200 > 100 → 'too many connections' errors. Use a proxy like PgBouncer.

resources.title: PgBouncer
resources.note: The standard connection pooler for Postgres.

resources.title: About connection pool sizing
resources.note: Why a *small* pool is usually faster. Counter-intuitive and important.

stages.title: Auth & Security
stages.summary: Prove who users are, control what they can do, and keep attackers out.

quiz.q: Authorization answers which question?
options: Are you allowed to do this?
quiz.explain: Authentication = who you are; authorization = what you're permitted to do.

resources.title: Authorization cheat sheet
resources.note: OWASP's practical checklist.

quiz.q: A JWT is trustworthy because…
options: It's signed, so tampering is detectable
quiz.explain: A JWT is signed; if anyone changes the payload the signature no longer validates. (Note: signed ≠ encrypted.)

quiz.q: Can you put a user's secret API key inside a JWT payload?
options: No — the payload is just base64 and anyone can read it
quiz.explain: JWTs are signed, not encrypted. The payload is trivially decodable — never put secrets in it.

resources.title: jwt.io — decode & inspect tokens
resources.note: Paste a JWT and see exactly how readable it is.

resources.title: Stop using JWT for sessions
resources.note: The classic counter-argument. Read it before defaulting to JWT.

quiz.q: OAuth 2.0 is primarily about…
quiz.explain: OAuth lets a user grant an app limited access to their account elsewhere without sharing their password.

quiz.q: Which flow should a single-page app use today?
quiz.explain: The implicit flow is deprecated. SPAs and mobile apps use Authorization Code with PKCE.

resources.title: OAuth 2.0 Simplified
resources.note: Aaron Parecki's free book — the friendliest OAuth resource there is.

quiz.q: How should passwords be stored?
quiz.explain: Never store plaintext. Use a slow, salted hash like bcrypt or argon2 so leaks are hard to crack.

quiz.q: Why is SHA-256 a bad choice for passwords?
options: It's too fast — a GPU can try billions of guesses per second
quiz.explain: SHA-256 is a fine hash but designed for speed. Password hashing must be deliberately slow and memory-hard.

resources.title: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
resources.note: OWASP's exact recommended parameters. Follow this.

resources.title: How to safely store a password
Have I Been Pwned
resources.note: Check passwords against known breaches (k-anonymity API).

quiz.q: SQL injection is prevented mainly by…
quiz.explain: Parameterized (prepared) queries separate code from data so user input can't become SQL.

quiz.q: GET /orders/123 returns any user's order to any logged-in user. What vulnerability is this?
quiz.explain: IDOR — you authenticated the user but never checked they own the resource. The most common real-world breach.

resources.title: OWASP Top 10
resources.note: The source. Bookmark it.

resources.title: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series
resources.note: Practical, per-topic defensive guidance.

resources.title: PortSwigger Web Security Academy
resources.note: Free labs where you actually exploit these bugs. Best security training online.

quiz.q: CORS is enforced by…
quiz.explain: CORS is a browser mechanism: the browser blocks cross-origin responses unless the server opts in via headers.

quiz.q: Your API works in Postman but fails with a CORS error in the browser. Where's the fix?
options: On the server — send the right Access-Control-Allow-* headers
quiz.explain: CORS is granted by the server. The frontend cannot fix it — the API must send the headers.

resources.title: CORS — MDN
resources.note: The definitive reference, including preflight.

resources.title: Will it CORS?
resources.note: Answers 'why is my request being blocked?' step by step.

quiz.q: You accidentally committed an API key, then deleted it in the next commit. Is it safe?
options: No — it lives in git history forever. You must rotate the key
Yes, if the repo is private
quiz.explain: Git history is permanent and may already be cloned/scraped. The only real fix is to rotate (revoke and reissue) the secret.

quiz.q: In a TLS handshake, the certificate proves…
options: The server really is who it claims to be (vouched for by a CA)
quiz.explain: The cert binds a public key to a domain, signed by a trusted Certificate Authority — that's the authenticity guarantee.

resources.title: How HTTPS works (comic)
resources.note: TLS handshake explained without the pain.

resources.title: SSL Labs server test
resources.note: Grade your own site's TLS config.

stages.title: Beyond Basics
stages.summary: Make backends fast and observable: caching, queues, jobs, config, logging, and metrics.

quiz.q: A cache mainly improves…
options: Latency by avoiding repeated expensive work
quiz.explain: Caching stores results of expensive operations so repeat requests are served fast from memory.

quiz.q: A popular key expires and 1,000 requests hit the DB at once. What's this called?
quiz.explain: A cache stampede (thundering herd). Mitigate with a lock/single-flight, or by staggering TTLs.

quiz.q: Why is 'write-through' caching safer than 'write-behind'?
options: The cache and DB are updated together, so a crash can't lose the write
quiz.explain: Write-behind buffers writes in the cache and flushes later — a crash loses them. Write-through commits to the DB synchronously.

quiz.q: The safest default for keeping caches from going permanently stale is…
quiz.explain: A TTL is your safety net: even if invalidation logic has a bug, stale entries eventually expire on their own.

resources.title: Caching patterns
resources.note: Cache-aside, write-through, write-behind, compared properly.

resources.title: Scaling Memcache at Facebook
resources.note: A famous paper on cache invalidation at extreme scale.

quiz.q: Why put slow work on a queue?
options: To respond fast now and process later
quiz.explain: Queues let you acknowledge quickly and process heavy tasks asynchronously with workers.

quiz.q: In pub/sub, a published message goes to…
quiz.explain: Pub/sub broadcasts to all subscribers. A queue gives each message to exactly one consumer.

resources.title: RabbitMQ tutorials
resources.note: Excellent hands-on tutorials in many languages.

quiz.q: You run 5 instances of your app, each with a cron that emails invoices at 9am. What happens?
options: Every customer gets 5 emails — you need a distributed lock or a leader
quiz.explain: Every instance runs the cron. Use a distributed lock, a leader election, or a dedicated scheduler so it fires once.

quiz.q: What is a dead-letter queue for?
options: Parking messages that failed repeatedly, for a human to inspect
quiz.explain: After N failed retries, move the message to a DLQ instead of retrying forever — then alert and investigate.

resources.title: Exponential backoff and jitter
resources.note: AWS Builders' Library — how to retry without killing your own service.

resources.title: Crontab guru
resources.note: Stop guessing at cron expressions.

quiz.q: Where should secrets like API keys live?
options: In environment variables / a secrets manager
quiz.explain: Config and secrets belong in the environment, never committed to source control.

resources.title: The Twelve-Factor App
resources.note: The canonical guide to config. Short and worth reading in full.

quiz.q: The 'three pillars' of observability are…
quiz.explain: Logs (events), metrics (numbers over time), and traces (request paths) together explain system behavior.

quiz.q: Why track p95/p99 latency instead of the average?
options: Averages hide the slow tail that real users actually feel
quiz.explain: An average can look fine while 5% of users wait 10 seconds. Percentiles expose the pain.

resources.title: OpenTelemetry — get started
resources.note: Instrument once, stay vendor-neutral. Start here.

resources.title: Google SRE Book — Monitoring
resources.note: The four golden signals, from the source. Free to read.

stages.title: Architecture & Scaling
stages.summary: Pro territory: how backends grow to handle millions of users without falling over.

quiz.q: Horizontal scaling means…
quiz.explain: Horizontal scaling adds more servers behind a load balancer; vertical scaling makes one server bigger.

quiz.q: What must be true before you can scale horizontally?
quiz.explain: If a server keeps sessions/files/cache in its own memory, requests routed elsewhere break. Push state to shared services.

resources.title: The System Design Primer
resources.note: The most popular free system-design resource on GitHub. Start here.

quiz.q: A load balancer's job is to…
quiz.explain: It spreads incoming traffic across many backend instances and routes around unhealthy ones.

quiz.q: Behind a load balancer, how do you get the real client IP?
options: Read the X-Forwarded-For header (and trust the proxy)
quiz.explain: Otherwise every request appears to come from the LB — breaking rate limiting and logging.

quiz.q: With hash(key) % N, what happens when you add one server?
options: Almost every key remaps to a different server
quiz.explain: Changing N changes the modulo for nearly every key — a catastrophic cache flush. Consistent hashing fixes exactly this.

quiz.q: What are 'virtual nodes' for in consistent hashing?
options: Spreading each server across many ring positions so load is even
quiz.explain: One point per server distributes unevenly. Placing each server at many points on the ring smooths the load.

resources.title: Consistent hashing explained
resources.note: Clear walkthrough with diagrams.

resources.title: Dynamo: Amazon's key-value store
resources.note: The paper that popularized consistent hashing in industry.

quiz.q: A common downside of microservices is…
quiz.explain: Splitting into services adds network calls, distributed failures, and ops overhead — real costs to weigh.

quiz.q: What should most small teams start with?
quiz.explain: A modular monolith keeps deploys simple while enforcing clean boundaries you can extract later — if you ever need to.

resources.title: MonolithFirst
resources.note: Martin Fowler on why starting with microservices usually fails.

quiz.q: The main benefit of emitting an 'OrderPlaced' event instead of calling three services directly is…
options: The producer doesn't need to know who consumes it — services stay decoupled
It removes the need for a database
quiz.explain: The order service just announces what happened. New consumers can be added later without touching it.

quiz.q: A key downside of event-driven systems is…
options: Harder to trace and reason about the overall flow
quiz.explain: Control flow becomes implicit and spread across services — you need good tracing and clear event contracts.

resources.title: What do you mean by 'Event-Driven'?
resources.note: Fowler disentangles the four things people mean by this term.

quiz.q: An API gateway handles…
options: Cross-cutting concerns at the edge: auth, rate limiting, routing
quiz.explain: It's the single front door: authenticate once, rate limit, route, and stop each service from reimplementing all that.

quiz.q: A service mesh mainly manages…
options: Traffic between your internal services (retries, mTLS, tracing)
quiz.explain: A mesh moves service-to-service concerns (mTLS, retries, circuit breaking, telemetry) into sidecars, out of your app code.

resources.title: Backends for Frontends (BFF)
resources.note: Sam Newman on tailoring a gateway per client.

resources.title: Istio — what is a service mesh?

quiz.q: Sharding splits data by…
quiz.explain: Sharding partitions a dataset across nodes (e.g. by user id) so no single DB holds everything.

quiz.q: A user updates their profile, reloads, and sees the OLD data. Likely cause?
options: Replication lag — the read hit a replica that hasn't caught up
quiz.explain: Classic read-after-write problem. Route a user's reads to the primary briefly after they write.

resources.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
resources.note: THE book for this stage. Chapters 5–6 cover replication and partitioning definitively.

quiz.q: An idempotent operation…
options: Has the same effect if repeated
quiz.explain: Idempotency means retrying the same request (e.g. with an idempotency key) won't double-charge or duplicate.

quiz.q: Because network partitions are unavoidable, CAP really forces you to choose between…
quiz.explain: You must tolerate partitions, so the real question is: when the network splits, do you refuse to answer (C) or answer possibly-stale data (A)?

resources.title: Please stop calling databases CP or AP
resources.note: Kleppmann on why the CAP framing is often misused.

resources.title: Stripe — idempotent requests
resources.note: The canonical real-world implementation of idempotency keys.

stages.title: DevOps & Deployment
stages.summary: Ship it reliably: containers, pipelines, deployment strategies, infrastructure as code, and the cloud.

quiz.q: A container mainly guarantees…
options: The same environment everywhere it runs
quiz.explain: Containers package your app with its dependencies so 'works on my machine' becomes 'works everywhere'.

quiz.q: Why COPY package.json and install deps BEFORE copying your source code?
options: Layer caching — deps rarely change, so that expensive layer stays cached
quiz.explain: A changed layer invalidates every layer after it. Copying source first would reinstall all dependencies on every build.

resources.title: Play with Docker
resources.note: A free Docker sandbox in the browser.

quiz.q: Kubernetes primarily helps you…
quiz.explain: Kubernetes schedules, scales, and restarts containers across a cluster automatically.

quiz.q: A failing readinessProbe causes Kubernetes to…
options: Stop sending it traffic, but leave it running
quiz.explain: Readiness controls traffic; liveness controls restarts. Confusing them causes restart loops or traffic sent to broken pods.

resources.title: Kubernetes the Hard Way
resources.note: Build a cluster from scratch to truly understand it.

quiz.q: Continuous Integration mostly means…
quiz.explain: CI runs your build and tests on every commit so problems surface immediately, before they pile up.

resources.title: Continuous Integration
resources.note: Fowler's original essay defining the practice.

quiz.q: Which strategy lets you test on 5% of real traffic before full rollout?
quiz.explain: A canary release routes a small slice of traffic to the new version so you catch problems with few users affected.

quiz.q: You need to rename a database column with zero downtime. What's the safe path?
options: Rename it in one migration during deploy
Expand: add the new column, write to both, backfill, switch reads, then drop the old one
Rename it in the ORM only
quiz.explain: The expand/contract pattern. During a rolling deploy, old and new code run simultaneously — the schema must satisfy both.

resources.title: Expand/contract migrations
resources.note: How to change a schema/API without breaking anything mid-deploy.

quiz.q: The main benefit of Infrastructure as Code is…
options: Infrastructure becomes versioned, reviewable, and reproducible
It removes the need for the cloud
quiz.explain: Clicking around a console isn't repeatable or reviewable. IaC puts infra in git — diffable, auditable, and rebuildable.

quiz.q: What is 'configuration drift'?
options: Real infrastructure no longer matches the code, because someone changed it by hand
quiz.explain: Manual console edits cause drift. That's why IaC tools have a plan/diff step — and why you don't hand-edit production.

quiz.q: A reverse proxy (like Nginx) sits…
options: In front of your app servers
quiz.explain: A reverse proxy fronts your servers to handle TLS, routing, caching, and load distribution.

resources.title: Caddy — automatic HTTPS
resources.note: The easiest reverse proxy to start with — HTTPS with zero config.

stages.title: System Design
stages.summary: Put it all together. Reason about real systems the way senior engineers do in interviews and on the job.

quiz.q: Which component makes short-code lookups fast at scale?
options: A cache in front of the database
quiz.explain: Reads dominate; a cache (e.g. Redis) in front of the key-value store keeps redirects fast.

quiz.q: Why is Base62-encoding a unique ID better than hashing the URL?
options: Collisions become structurally impossible — no check-and-retry needed
quiz.explain: Each ID is unique, so each code is unique by construction. Hashing requires a uniqueness check on every write.

resources.title: ByteByteGo — system design
resources.note: Excellent visual system-design breakdowns.

resources.title: Snowflake IDs
resources.note: How to generate unique, time-sortable IDs across many servers.

quiz.q: The token bucket algorithm allows…
options: Short bursts up to the bucket size
quiz.explain: Token bucket refills at a steady rate but lets clients spend saved tokens for brief bursts.

quiz.q: Why does `GET count; if ok: SET count+1` fail as a rate limiter?
options: It's a race condition — two servers can both read the same count and both pass
quiz.explain: Check-then-act isn't atomic. Use a Lua script or an atomic INCR so the read+write happen as one operation.

resources.title: How Stripe rate-limits
resources.note: A real production design, with code.

quiz.q: Real-time chat delivery typically relies on…
quiz.explain: Persistent WebSocket connections plus a broker/fan-out layer deliver messages instantly to recipients.

quiz.q: Alice is on server-1 and Bob is on server-3. How does Alice's message reach Bob?
options: Via a shared pub/sub broker that server-3 subscribes to for Bob
quiz.explain: Direct server-to-server is O(n²) and brittle. A pub/sub backbone decouples them entirely.

resources.title: How Discord stores billions of messages
resources.note: A real, readable engineering deep-dive.

quiz.q: 'Fan-out on write' means…
options: Computing the feed when the user opens the app
Pushing a new post into every follower's feed at post time
quiz.explain: Fan-out on write precomputes feeds so reads are instant — but a celebrity with 50M followers makes one post cost 50M writes.

quiz.q: How do real systems handle celebrity accounts?
options: A hybrid: fan-out on write for normal users, fan-out on read for celebrities
quiz.explain: Celebrity posts are pulled at read time and merged into the precomputed feed — avoiding the massive write amplification.

resources.title: Twitter's timeline at scale
resources.note: The classic fan-out discussion, including the celebrity problem.

quiz.q: Eventual consistency means…
options: Replicas converge to the same value over time
quiz.explain: Under eventual consistency, replicas may briefly differ but converge once updates propagate.

quiz.q: Why must you add 'jitter' to exponential backoff?
options: So all clients don't retry at the same instant and re-crash the service
quiz.explain: Without randomness, every client retries in sync — a thundering herd that kills the service the moment it recovers.

resources.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
resources.note: If you read one backend book in your life, make it this one.

resources.title: AWS Builders' Library
resources.note: How Amazon actually builds resilient systems — timeouts, retries, health checks.

quiz.q: What's the first step when something is 'slow'?
options: Measure to find the actual bottleneck
quiz.explain: Optimizing without measuring is guessing. Profile first — the bottleneck is almost never where you assume.

quiz.q: Amdahl's law implies that…
options: Speedup is capped by the part you can't optimize
quiz.explain: If 90% of time is one DB query, making everything else infinitely fast gains you only 10%. Fix the dominant cost.

resources.title: Latency numbers every programmer should know
resources.note: Memorize the orders of magnitude. It changes how you design.

resources.title: Brendan Gregg — systems performance
resources.note: The USE method and flame graphs, from the master.

quiz.q: The base of the test pyramid is…
quiz.explain: Most tests should be fast, focused unit tests; fewer integration and E2E tests sit above them.

quiz.q: Which test most reliably prevents a real security breach?
options: Asserting user A cannot read user B's data
quiz.explain: Broken access control (IDOR) is the #1 real-world vulnerability. Test authorization explicitly.

resources.title: Testcontainers
resources.note: Run a real database in Docker for your integration tests.

stages.title: Expert Track
stages.summary: The deep end. What's actually happening inside your database, and the patterns senior engineers reach for.

lessons.title: Database Internals: B-Trees, LSM & the WAL

quiz.q: Why are B-trees good for reads but LSM-trees better for heavy writes?
options: LSM buffers writes in memory and flushes sequentially, avoiding random disk writes
quiz.explain: B-trees update in place (random I/O). LSM-trees append to memory then flush sorted runs sequentially — much faster writes, at the cost of read amplification.

quiz.q: What does the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) guarantee?
options: Durability — a committed transaction survives a crash because it was logged before being applied
quiz.explain: The WAL is written and fsynced before the change is applied. On crash, the DB replays the log. That's the 'D' in ACID.

resources.title: Database Internals (book)
resources.note: Alex Petrov's deep dive into storage engines. The definitive text.

resources.title: How does a database work? (Let's Build a Simple Database)
resources.note: Build a database from scratch in C — the best way to truly get it.

quiz.q: Optimistic locking works by…
options: Checking a version number on write and failing if it changed
quiz.explain: Optimistic concurrency assumes conflicts are rare: read a version, and on update assert it's unchanged. If it changed, retry.

quiz.q: The classic way to avoid deadlocks between transactions is…
options: Always acquire locks in a consistent order
quiz.explain: Deadlock happens when A locks row 1 then 2, while B locks row 2 then 1. A consistent global lock ordering makes that impossible.

resources.title: MVCC explained
resources.note: How Postgres lets readers and writers avoid blocking each other.

resources.title: Martin Kleppmann — how to do distributed locking
resources.note: Why naive Redis locks are unsafe. A masterclass in rigorous thinking.

quiz.q: In event sourcing, the source of truth is…
options: The immutable append-only log of events that produced the state
quiz.explain: You store every event ever (Deposited, Withdrew). Current state is derived by replaying them — giving you a perfect audit log and time travel.

quiz.q: CQRS separates…
options: The write model from the read model
quiz.explain: Commands (writes) and Queries (reads) get different models, so each can be optimized independently — often with different databases.

resources.title: Event Sourcing
resources.note: Fowler's original definition.

resources.title: CQRS
resources.note: Including his warnings about when NOT to use it.

resources.title: Turning the database inside out
resources.note: Kleppmann's famous talk — a genuinely mind-expanding read.

quiz.q: A Bloom filter can tell you…
options: Definitely absent, or *probably* present (false positives possible, never false negatives)
quiz.explain: That one-sided error is the whole trick: a 'no' is certain, so you can skip an expensive lookup entirely. A 'maybe' costs you one check.

quiz.q: Why use HyperLogLog to count unique visitors?
options: It estimates cardinality of billions of items in ~12KB instead of storing every ID
quiz.explain: Exact counting needs a set of every ID (gigabytes). HLL trades ~2% error for constant, tiny memory.

resources.title: Bloom filters by example
resources.note: Play with one in the browser — it clicks immediately.

resources.title: HyperLogLog explained
resources.note: By Redis's creator, antirez.

quiz.q: A 'bounded context' is…
options: An explicit boundary within which a model and its terms have one consistent meaning
quiz.explain: 'Customer' means something different to Billing than to Support. Bounded contexts let each own its model instead of forcing one global schema.

quiz.q: Why does DDD matter for microservices?
options: Bounded contexts are the natural, correct seams to split services along
quiz.explain: Splitting by technical layer creates chatty, coupled services. Splitting by bounded context gives services that own their data and change independently.

resources.title: Domain-Driven Design Quickly (free book)
resources.note: A free 100-page distillation of Eric Evans' work.
